title: The Traditional Latin Mass
summary: The ancient Roman Rite codified by Pope St. Pius V after the Council of Trent.
history: The Traditional Latin Mass (also called the Tridentine Mass, Usus Antiquior, or Extraordinary Form) is the form of the Roman Rite codified by Pope St. Pius V in 1570 following the Council of Trent. However, the Mass itself is far older — its Canon (Eucharistic Prayer) dates to at least the 4th century, and many elements are apostolic in origin. Pope Benedict XVI declared in 2007 (Summorum Pontificum) that this Mass was 'never abrogated.' It was the universal form of the Roman Rite for over 400 years until the liturgical reforms of 1969-70.
explanation: The TLM is offered in Latin (the sacred language of the Roman Church) with the priest facing liturgical east (ad orientem), toward God. The Mass is the unbloody re-presentation of Christ's sacrifice on Calvary — not merely a memorial meal. Key features include: the Prayers at the Foot of the Altar, the Offertory prayers expressing the sacrificial nature of the Mass, the Roman Canon (a single unchanging Eucharistic Prayer), Communion received kneeling and on the tongue, and the Last Gospel (the Prologue of St. John's Gospel).
practice: Attend the Traditional Latin Mass on all Sundays and Holy Days of Obligation. Arrive before the Introit and remain through the Last Gospel. Follow along with a hand missal. The faithful participate through interior prayer and meditation, uniting themselves with the priest's offering of the Holy Sacrifice.
churchTeaching: Council of Trent, Session XXII: 'The holy Synod teaches that this sacrifice is truly propitiatory.' Pope Benedict XVI, Summorum Pontificum (2007): 'The Roman Missal promulgated by St. Pius V was never juridically abrogated.'

title: The 1962 Missal
summary: The most commonly used edition of the Traditional Latin Mass today.
history: The 1962 Missal was the last edition published before the Second Vatican Council. It incorporates the rubrical changes of Pope John XXIII's 1960 Code of Rubrics, which simplified certain ceremonies and reduced the number of octaves and vigils. This is the edition authorized by Summorum Pontificum (2007) and is used by the FSSP, ICKSP, and most diocesan TLM communities worldwide.
explanation: Key differences from earlier editions: the rubrics were simplified (fewer genuflections, simplified calendar), most octaves were suppressed (only Christmas, Easter, and Pentecost retained), the Confiteor before Communion was removed, St. Joseph was added to the Canon ('cum beato Ioseph'), and the ranking system for feasts was simplified from the older system of Doubles, Semi-doubles, and Simples.
practice: This is the default missal for most traditional Catholics today. If your parish or chapel uses the 1962 Missal, your hand missal should match this edition. The FSSP, ICKSP, and most diocesan-approved TLM communities use this form exclusively.
churchTeaching: Pope Benedict XVI, Summorum Pontificum (2007) established the 1962 Missal as the 'Extraordinary Form' of the Roman Rite.

title: The 1955 Missal
summary: Post-Holy Week reform but pre-1960 rubrical changes.
history: In 1955, Pope Pius XII promulgated a reform of the Holy Week liturgy, restoring the Easter Vigil to its ancient nighttime celebration (it had been moved to Saturday morning by the late Middle Ages). The 1955 Missal retains the pre-1960 rubrics for the rest of the liturgical year, including the full system of octaves, vigils, and the older feast ranking system (Doubles of the First and Second Class, Semi-doubles, Simples).
explanation: The 1955 edition represents a middle ground: it includes the restored Holy Week (widely praised for returning the Vigil to night) but retains the older rubrical complexity throughout the rest of the year. The Double Confiteor before Communion is retained. Some traditional communities prefer this edition as a balance between the older richness and the 1955 Holy Week improvements.
practice: Used by some traditional communities that accept the Holy Week reform but prefer the pre-1960 rubrics. If your community uses this form, consult your priest about which specific practices differ from the 1962 edition.

title: The Pre-1955 Missal
summary: The Missal with the original Holy Week and full octave system.
history: Before the 1955 Holy Week reforms, the Triduum ceremonies had their ancient form: Palm Sunday included an elaborate blessing and procession of palms, Holy Thursday's Mass was in the morning, Good Friday's liturgy was in the morning, and the Easter Vigil was celebrated Saturday morning (having migrated from its original nighttime celebration over centuries). The full system of octaves was intact — many more feasts had eight-day celebrations, and the complex calendar of Doubles, Semi-doubles, and Simples governed feast ranking.
explanation: Some traditional Catholics prefer this form because it represents the Mass as it was celebrated for centuries before any 20th-century changes. The original Holy Week, while celebrated at different times than the early Church intended, had accumulated rich ceremonial traditions over many centuries. The full octave system meant that great feasts like Epiphany, Corpus Christi, and the Assumption were celebrated for eight consecutive days.
practice: Used by some SSPX chapels and independent traditional communities. The most notable practical difference is the Holy Week liturgy: the Easter Vigil is on Saturday morning rather than Saturday night. If your community uses this form, a pre-1955 hand missal is essential.
